explanation: The LTL formula states that if the system transitions into 'Start-up Mode' (i.e., it is currently not in 'Start-up Mode' but will be in the next state), then the 'powerButton' must be pressed at the current state. This captures the requirement that pushing the power button is a necessary condition for the 'Start-up Mode' transition, acting as the explicit mechanism that 'allows' it. It ensures that entry into 'Start-up Mode' does not occur without the 'powerButton' being active at the point of transition.

The natural language statement "The transition to Start-up Mode shall be allowed by pushing the power button" implies that entering `StartUpMode` is conditional upon `powerButton` being pressed. Specifically, it means that `StartUpMode` cannot become active unless `powerButton` has been pressed. The LTL formula `G(StartUpMode -> (!StartUpMode U powerButton))` captures this by stating: 'Globally, if the system is in `StartUpMode`, then it must be the case that `StartUpMode` was false until `powerButton` became true (and `powerButton` eventually became true).'. This ensures that `powerButton` acts as the necessary enabler for the system to enter `StartUpMode`. If `StartUpMode` becomes true, `powerButton` must have been pressed at the moment of transition or just before it, with `StartUpMode` being false in the preceding states.

The natural language statement specifies a necessary condition for the system to enter "Start-up Mode": it must be initiated by pushing the "power button" while the system is "off". The LTL formula `G ( (X StartUpMode & !StartUpMode) -> (powerButton & off) )` captures this requirement. It states that globally (`G`), if `StartUpMode` becomes true in the next state (`X StartUpMode`) and was false in the current state (`!StartUpMode`), implying a transition into `StartUpMode`, then in that current state, both `powerButton` must be pressed and the system must be `off`. This correctly models the "shall be allowed by" clause, ensuring that any transition to `StartUpMode` is conditional on these specific preconditions.

The LTL property "G (powerButton -> F StartUpMode)" asserts that globally, whenever the power button is pressed (`powerButton`), it will eventually lead to the system being in Start-up Mode (`F StartUpMode`). This directly translates the natural language statement "The transition to Start-up Mode shall be allowed by pushing the power button" by establishing that pushing the power button is the enabling action for the system to eventually enter its start-up phase. The 'F' (eventually) operator accounts for any potential intermediate steps or delays in the transition process.

The natural language statement specifies that a transition into 'Start-up Mode' is only permitted if the 'powerButton' is pressed. This means that for any instance where the system enters 'Start-up Mode' (i.e., 'StartUpMode' is false in the current state and becomes true in the next state), the 'powerButton' must be active in the current state. The LTL formula `G((!StartUpMode & X StartUpMode) -> powerButton)` captures this by stating that, globally, if there is a transition where `StartUpMode` becomes true, then `powerButton` must have been true in the immediately preceding state.

This LTL property captures the requirement that pushing the power button is the mechanism that allows the system to transition into Start-up Mode from an off state. The formula G((off & powerButton) -> X F StartUpMode) means: Globally (G), if the system is currently off (off) and the power button is pressed (powerButton), then in the next state (X), it is eventually (F) guaranteed that the system will enter Start-up Mode (StartUpMode). This accurately reflects the causal relationship and the implied initial state of being 'off' before turning the system 'on' into 'StartUpMode'. The use of 'X F' ensures that the StartUpMode is entered at some point in the future after the button press, which is appropriate for a mode transition that may not be instantaneous.

The natural language statement specifies that pushing the power button allows the transition to Start-up Mode when the system is off. This implies a cause-and-effect relationship: if the system is in the 'off' state and the 'powerButton' is pressed, then the system must eventually enter 'StartUpMode'. The LTL formula `G((off & powerButton) -> F StartUpMode)` captures this by stating that globally (G), whenever the system is off (`off`) AND the power button is pressed (`powerButton`), it will eventually (F) transition into Start-up Mode (`StartUpMode`). This ensures that the specified action (pushing the power button while off) leads to the desired state (Start-up Mode).

The natural language statement specifies that the transition to 'Start-up Mode' is enabled by pressing the 'powerButton'. It implies that when the system is in an 'off' state and the power button is pushed, the system should eventually enter 'Start-up Mode'. The LTL formula captures this by stating that globally, if the system is 'off' and the 'powerButton' is pressed simultaneously, then 'StartUpMode' will eventually become true.

The LTL property states that if the system transitions into 'Start-up Mode' (i.e., 'StartUpMode' was false in the current state and becomes true in the next state), then the 'powerButton' must be pressed in the current state. This accurately captures the requirement that the transition to Start-up Mode is 'allowed by' or enabled by pushing the power button. It ensures that the system cannot enter Start-up Mode without the power button being active at the point of transition.

The property states that globally, if the system is currently in the 'off' state and the 'powerButton' is pressed, then eventually the system will transition to 'StartUpMode'. This captures the meaning of 'turning it on' (from 'off' state) and the 'powerButton' being the trigger that 'allows' the transition to 'Start-up Mode'.
